ocar.opportunity: No complete map of any animal nervous system existed at the level of individual neurons and synapses, making it impossible to reason about how neural circuits generate behavior from first principles.
ocar.challenge: Reconstructing an entire nervous system required imaging thousands of serial ultrathin sections by transmission electron microscopy, then manually tracing every neuronal process and synapse — a task of unprecedented scale that took over a decade of painstaking work.
ocar.action: White, Southgate, Thomson, and Brenner reconstructed the complete nervous system of C. elegans hermaphrodites from serial-section EM, tracing all 302 neurons and cataloguing approximately 5,000 chemical synapses, 2,000 neuromuscular junctions, and 600 gap junctions.
ocar.resolution: The study produced the first and (for decades) only complete connectome of any organism, revealing an essentially invariant wiring pattern across individuals, a modular organization of neuron classes, and stereotyped synaptic connectivity that provided the foundation for all subsequent C. elegans circuit analysis.
ocar.future_work: Whether the invariant wiring of C. elegans holds for organisms with larger, more plastic nervous systems, and how the connectome maps to the animal's full behavioral repertoire, remain enduring questions.

plain_language_summary: In a project that took over a decade, scientists photographed thin slices of the tiny worm C. elegans under an electron microscope and manually traced every single neuron and connection. With only 302 neurons, it was the first animal to have its complete nervous system wired map made — a landmark achievement that all modern connectomics is built on.

summaries.beginner: The roundworm C. elegans has exactly 302 neurons, making its brain small enough to map completely. This 1986 paper did exactly that using an electron microscope, tracing every neuron and every connection by hand. It was the first complete nervous-system wiring diagram ever created and launched the field of connectomics.
summaries.intermediate: White et al. (1986) established the conceptual and methodological foundation for connectomics by reconstructing the complete C. elegans nervous system from serial TEM sections. The 302-neuron hermaphrodite connectome revealed 118 neuron classes with stereotyped morphology and connectivity, an invariant wiring pattern across individuals, and a modular organization. This work defined the paradigm of using structural connectivity to understand neural circuit function.
summaries.advanced: This foundational work required manual reconstruction of thousands of serial ultrathin sections, a strategy that is computationally automated in modern connectomics. The C. elegans connectome revealed key principles: synaptic connectivity is largely invariant across individuals (unlike vertebrates), neurons make en passant synapses along longitudinal process bundles, and gap junctions constitute a significant fraction of connectivity (~10%). Limitations of the original reconstruction — since revised with additional synapses identified in later re-analysis — illustrate that even 'complete' connectomes require iterative proofreading.

discussion_prompts: The C. elegans connectome was largely invariant across individuals — how does this compare to inter-individual variability observed in more recently reconstructed connectomes, and what does it imply for the relationship between structure and behavior?
How did the manual reconstruction methods used in 1986 compare in accuracy to modern automated segmentation, and what have subsequent re-analyses of the C. elegans connectome corrected?
Given that we now have the complete C. elegans connectome and can genetically manipulate individual neurons, are there still unanswered questions about how its circuits generate behavior?
